settings.title: Three Cheap Fixes That Help a House Sell
settings.excerpt: You do not need a renovation to make a strong first impression, just a focused weekend of small jobs.
settings.body:
Buyers decide how they feel about a home within seconds of walking in. The good news is that the things shaping that first impression are usually small and inexpensive.

Sort the front door. A clean door, a working bell, and a tidy step set the tone before anyone steps inside. It is the first thing a buyer touches, and a stiff lock or a faded coat of paint plants doubt before they have even seen a room.

Let the light in. Clean windows and open curtains make rooms feel larger and more cared for. Light is the cheapest improvement there is, and a bright room almost always feels bigger than a dim one of the same size.

Clear the surfaces. A buyer needs space to imagine their own life, and that is hard among someone else's clutter.

A focused weekend

None of this needs a tradesperson or a big budget. It needs a couple of free days and a willingness to look at your own home the way a stranger would. Walk in through the front door as a buyer might and notice what your eye lands on first.

  • Friday evening, clear the clutter from every surface
  • Saturday, clean the windows and sort out the front door
  • Sunday, do a final walk through as if you were viewing it

Showing as well as it lives

None of this costs much. It simply helps a home show as well as it actually lives. Buyers are not really judging the clutter or the grubby door. They are deciding whether they can picture themselves there, and a clean, light, uncluttered home makes that picture far easier to form.
